retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: By directly reducing and eliminating the cytoplasmic apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA concentration, mipomersen plays a crucial role in lowering the ribosomal production of the final, folded apolipoprotein B-100 polypeptide. This therapy ultimately leads to decreased production of protein human apolipoprotein B-100 and, therefore, reduces the production of low-density lipoprotein and lower-low-density lipoprotein cholesterol. Mipomersen functioned consistently across all species in tested human volunteers and patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates can raise HDL and lower triglycerides by stimulating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-alpha (PPARα). A novel selective PPARα modulator, pemafibrate, effectively lowers triglycerides and apoC-III. Unlike older fibrates, which have limited PPARα activation due to their nonselectivity, pemafibrate is 2500 times more potent than fenofibrate in activating PPARα activation. [47] The large-scale PROMINENT trial demonstrated significant reductions in triglycerides, VLDL, remnant cholesterol, and apoC-III, although it did not improve cardiac outcomes, which was the trial's primary end point. [47] [52] [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A promising new area in lipid-lowering therapies is that of antisense oligonucleotides to target mRNA of proteins that are involved in cholesterol metabolism. Initial trials of apoB antisense oligos have shown promise with a 50% reduction in Apo B levels, a 30% reduction in LDL levels, and decreased cardiovascular risk.  However, they cause liver dysfunction. A new goal of the 2018 NCEP guidelines is the inclusion of apoB, stating that an apoB greater than 130 mg/dL is a risk-enhancing factor and requires measurement in primary prevention treatment protocols, and this is an essential step towards the analysis of apoB for cardiovascular risk. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen is a 20-base pair, 2’-O-methoxyethyl modified antisense oligonucleotide complementary to human apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA. [3] Mipomersen aims to identify and hybridize with the human apolipoprotein B-100 mRNA via the traditional deoxyribonucleotide or ribonucleotide base pairing mechanism. The interface between the complementary mipomersen therapeutic oligonucleotide with the newly synthesized human apolipoprotein B-100 leads to the degradation of the messenger RNA. Most frequently, the hybridization of mipomersen to its target mRNA leads to the recruitment of RNase H1, a catalytic enzyme that degrades ribonucleic acid in the cellular cytoplasm. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action -- Distribution
retrieved_docs.content: Studies in mice and monkey experiments have shown that mipomersen dose-dependently reduces apolipoprotein B-100 concentrations and has no effect on high-density lipoprotein cholesterol levels (HDL-C). Further experimentations have demonstrated that mipomersen achieves steady-state tissue concentrations following the 6-months mark post-administration. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Apolipoprotein A acts as a surrogate marker of HDL plasma concentration, and evidence of the utility of the ApoB: Apo A ratio as a predictor of cardiovascular disease (CVD) has been reported, especially in the INTERHEART study. However, given the controversy about the role of HDL in preventing atherosclerosis, there is no strong indication to measure apoA.

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) and chylomicrons, thereby decreasing LDL-C levels in plasma. [11] Inhibition of VLDL synthesis decreases the secretion of apoB-100-containing lipoproteins. The reduction in lipoprotein(a) or Lp(a) levels is correlated with a significant decrease in major adverse cardiovascular events. Lomitapide administration has been associated with a significant reduction in lipoprotein(a) levels. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: ApoB-100 carrying particles such as LDL and lipoprotein (a) predispose to premature atherosclerotic disease (ASCVD).

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lomitapide could be beneficial for HoFH patients exhibiting insufficient response to PCSK9 inhibitor treatment, those with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and a baseline LDL-C of 190 mg/dL or higher, and those who demonstrate insufficient responses to statins, whether used alone or in combination with PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe. Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein and chylomicrons, thereby lowering plasma LDL-C levels. The safety and efficacy of lomitapide have not been established for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia. This activity elucidates the indications, mechanism of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, and other relevant interactions essential for interprofessional healthcare team members in treating patients with HoFH and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Familial defective apo B is an autosomal dominant disorder that arises due to a mutation in apo B that prevents binding of the defective apo B to the LDL receptor. This condition results in a clinical phenotype similar to classical familial hypercholesterolemia due to mutations in the LDL receptor with increased LDL-cholesterol, xanthomas, and premature ASCVD, underscoring the importance of apoB in atherogenesis. The most frequent variant is apoB-3500, caused by a point mutation resulting in a substitution of glutamine for arginine at position 3500. [1] This mutation is an excellent illustration of a defect in an important ligand resulting in premature ASCVD.

retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The infusion of apolipoprotein A-I (apoA-I) has been investigated for its potential cardiovascular benefits, as apoA-I levels are strongly associated with a reduced risk of cardiovascular disease in patients already on statin therapy. [24] A large study examined the effects of consecutive weekly infusions of apoA-I versus placebo after a recent myocardial infarction to determine the benefits of apoA-I infusion therapy. [25] Patients who received the apoA-I infusion had an increase in cholesterol efflux capacity. However, further research is needed to determine if this therapy will translate to a reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen is a second-generation novel agent identified as an antisense oligonucleotide inhibitor of apolipoprotein B-100 (apoB-100). It is indicated in the management of homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lomitapide and mipomersen are antihyperlipidemic agents indicated for treating homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH). Lomitapide is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use alongside other antilipidemic drugs and a low-fat diet to lower serum lipids, decrease low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol (LDL-C), total cholesterol, apolipoprotein B (apo-B), and non–high-density lipoprotein cholesterol in patients with HoFH. Patients with HoFH might present with arcus corneae, tendinous xanthomas, xanthelasma, and planar or tuberous xanthomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypertriglyceridemia -- Treatment / Management -- Statins
retrieved_docs.content: Olezarsen, administered as a monthly injection, has recently received a "fast-track" designation from the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating familial chylomicronemia and has shown promise in managing FHTG. It is a synthetically modified DNA fragment that targets hepatocyte mRNA to inhibit apolipoprotein C-III (APOC3) synthesis, thus enhancing the clearance of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins. Clinical trials have demonstrated a reduction in plasma triglyceride levels by approximately 60% after 6 months. [37] [38] [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond the statin target approach for low-density lipoprotein (LDL), new treatments targeting triglyceride-rich lipoproteins have emerged and are advancing through clinical development. Biological and RNA-based agents now complement traditional small-molecule treatments, which have seen significant refinement. Innovative targeting strategies have enhanced the efficacy of these novel interventions while substantially improving their tolerability. [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Indications -- FDA Approved Use
retrieved_docs.content: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approves Mipomersen as an adjunct to current lipid-lowering therapy and diet modifications to limit low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), apolipoprotein B-100 (apoB), and non-high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol in patients diagnosed with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH). [1] [2] A combination of therapies may be utilized for patients unable to achieve their target low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 948
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other lipid modifying agents in ATC” comprises a diverse group of drugs that are used to influence lipid metabolism. Included in this class are the small‑molecule agents inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, lomitapide, as well as the monoclonal antibodies alirocumab and evolocumab. Natural compounds policosanol and tiadenol are also members of this group, and both are identified as fatty alcohols (mastné alkoholy). Among the agents, ezetimib and bempedoic acid are specifically indicated for the treatment of type II hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني). This collection of lipid‑modifying therapies—ranging from novel RNA‑targeting drugs and PCSK9 inhibitors to traditional fatty‑alcohol supplements—illustrates the multiple pharmacologic strategies available for managing elevated lipid levels and reducing cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 34
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen, a lipid‑modifying agent, exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis and is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This ATC class also includes inclisiran, ezetimibe, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, lomitapide, policosanol and tiadenol. Both ezetimibe and bempedoic acid are indicated for the treatment of type II hyperlipidemia, highlighting the therapeutic focus of several members of this group on abnormal lipid metabolism. Policosanol and tiadenol are chemically related to fatty alcohols, reflecting a shared structural category within the broader lipid‑modifying spectrum. In a separate pharmacologic category, argatroban and dabigatran etexilate belong to the class of direct thrombin inhibitors, agents that target thrombin to prevent clot formation. The heterocyclic compound benzimidazole functions as an inhibitor of both dabigatran etexilate and lomitapide, indicating a potential interaction or modulatory role across distinct therapeutic classes—anticoagulation and lipid modification. Overall, the knowledge base links specific drugs to their mechanistic classes, therapeutic indications, and chemical relationships, providing a comprehensive view of how lipid‑lowering agents, anticoagulants, and related chemical entities intersect in clinical pharmacology and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 224
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiovascular pharmacotherapy domain includes lipid‑modifying agents, which are divided into a plain subgroup and other subgroups. Within the plain subgroup, Nicotinic acid and derivatives, lipid‑modifying, plain belong to the chemical family nicofuranose and are also linked to peripheral vasodilators; the broader category Nicotinic acid and derivatives encompasses these plain agents. Another plain subgroup, designated as Other lipid‑modifying agents in ATC, comprises inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, and lomitapide. This subgroup is associated with compounds such as policosanol and tiadenol, the latter mapping to fatty alcohols (mastné alkoholy). Both ezetimib and bempedoic acid are indicated for type II hyperlipidemia. Lomitapide, a benzimidazole‑based microsomal triglyceride transfer protein inhibitor, is used for severe hyperlipidaemia, illustrating the therapeutic use of the heterocyclic benzimidazole scaffold. Policosanol, a mixture of long‑chain aliphatic fatty alcohols derived from plant waxes, is employed as a dietary supplement to support cholesterol management. In addition to lipid‑modifying therapy, the cardiovascular drug class includes direct thrombin inhibitors, namely argatroban and dabigatran etexilát, the latter being chemically a benzimidazole; direct thrombin inhibitors provide anticoagulant effects. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates how lipid‑modifying agents—including nicotinic acid derivatives, inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, and lomitapide—are organized within cardiovascular treatment, how specific chemical families such as nicofuranose, benzimidazole, and fatty alcohols are leveraged to regulate lipid metabolism, and how direct thrombin inhibitors contribute to anticoagulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen, a member of the ATC class “Other lipid modifying agents,” exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis. The “Other lipid modifying agents” class also includes inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, alirocumab, evolocumab, lomitapide, policosanol, and tiadenol. Both ezetimib and bempedoic acid may be used to treat type II hyperlipidemia. Policosanol and tiadenol are chemically mapped to fatty alcohols. The class “Direct thrombin inhibitors” comprises argatroban and dabigatran etexilát. Dabigatran etexilát is identified as a benzimidazole derivative, and lomitapide is also classified under the benzimidazole chemical class. This network of class memberships, therapeutic indications, and biochemical effects illustrates how lipid‑modifying agents, anticoagulants, and their underlying chemical structures are interrelated, informing pharmacologic selection and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, which are classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their inclusion in the plain lipid‑modifying drug category underscores their primary role in managing hyperlipidemia and preventing related cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 779
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in cholesterol biosynthesis, which leads to reductions in low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and triglycerides and modest increases in high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are widely used to treat hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating class‑related benefits, and monitoring for shared adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrát is classified as a member of the “Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain” group, indicating that it belongs to the fibrate class of plain lipid‑modifying agents. As a fibrate, fenofibrát shares the typical pharmacologic actions of this drug family, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 848
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: فخذي (femoral bruit), and مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي (abnormal electrocardiogram); a diffuse form (التصلب العصيدي المعمم) is also described. Vascular ischemia and an increased risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion are associated with this condition, the latter also being linked to an atrial mucinous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Lipid‑lowering therapy targeting this atherosclerotic pathway includes agents in the ATC class “Other lipid modifying agents” such as inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, alirocumab, evolocumab, lomitapide, policosanol, and tiadenol; ezetimib and bempedoic acid are indicated for type II hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني). Preventive or therapeutic use of alirocumab, evolocumab, and inclisiran may halt progression of التصلب العصيدي, while bempedoic acid may be used for treatment. Surgical management of peripheral arterial disease involves peripheral vascular bypass procedures that connect the abdominal aorta to compromised peripheral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2741
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that are used to improve abnormal blood lipid profiles, particularly by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol. The primary members of this plain fibrate class are bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. All three agents act as agonists of peroxisome proliferator‑activated receptor‑α (PPAR‑α), leading to increased oxidation of fatty acids, reduced hepatic production of very‑low‑density lipoprotein, and enhanced clearance of triglyceride‑rich lipoproteins. Because of these mechanisms, bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát are employed in the management of hypertriglyceridaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, and certain cases of secondary dyslipidaemia, contributing to cardiovascular risk reduction when used appropriately in patient care.
